does: This paper identifies and measures temporal memory contamination, a failure mode where safety violations emerge as language model agents accumulate memories across multiple independent tasks over extended horizons. The authors develop a trigger-probe protocol that isolates memory effects by evaluating fixed probe tasks against read-only memory snapshots at varying lengths, using a NullMemory baseline to identify memory-induced safety failures. They apply this evaluation framework across three deployment scenarios involving realistic interactions with records, memos, forms, and email correspondence, systematically tracking how agent safety degrades as memory grows rather than measuring point-in-time safety on individual tasks.
connects: This work extends beyond existing within-task safety evaluations of memory-equipped LLM agents that focus on single-scenario robustness under adversarial conditions like prompt injection and memory poisoning. It builds on recent research examining how persistent context affects model behavior and responds to the gap between typical safety benchmarks—which evaluate stateless or short-horizon agents—and real deployment scenarios where agents continuously accumulate information. The paper connects to broader work on non-stationarity, distribution shift, and emergent failures in deployed AI systems, translating these concepts specifically to the temporal dimension of agentic memory management.
enables: This work opens investigation into systematic mitigation strategies for memory-induced safety degradation, such as memory pruning, refresh mechanisms, and bounded-context architectures that limit historical information exposure. It enables development of more realistic safety evaluation frameworks that reflect deployment conditions rather than isolated task performance. The trigger-probe protocol itself provides a reusable methodology for auditing other stateful systems. This research points toward temporal safety guarantees as a design consideration in agent architectures, informing how organizations should structure memory retention policies and safety monitoring systems for long-running deployed agents.
